title: The cd-indices of intervals in the uncrossing partial order on matchings
overall_summary: The paper studies the uncrossing poset P_n of complete matchings on 2n labeled points, ordered by resolving crossings, and gives recursive formulas for cd-indices and ab-indices of its intervals. Its main structural tool is an explicit order-reversing bijection from P_n to a certain induced subposet MP_n of affine Bruhat order, whose elements are modular palindromic affine permutations with a no-long-consecutive-subword condition. Using Reading's framework for Bruhat intervals, the paper proves that certain intervals in MP_n are obtained from a product with a two-element chain by a controlled sequence of zipping operations, and converts this into cd-index recursions. For the original problem, the most relevant content is the poset-level model for crossing matchings and their resolutions, the interval decomposition by products and zippings, and the possibility of treating cubical cell attachments coming from resolving crossings via uncrossing-poset intervals or subposets.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 3 basic facts about the uncrossing poset P_n
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let P_n be the set of complete matchings of [2n], i.e. partitions of [2n] into n two-element blocks. If a matching has blocks (i_r,j_r) and (i_s,j_s), written with i_r<j_r and i_s<j_s, these two blocks form a crossing when i_r<i_s<j_r<j_s. Resolving such a crossing means replacing the two crossing blocks by either (i_r,i_s),(j_r,j_s) or by (i_r,j_s),(i_s,j_r). The partial order P_n is generated by declaring a matching obtained by resolving a crossing of τ to lie below τ. If c(τ) denotes the number of crossings of τ, then P_n is graded of rank binom(n,2) with rank function c. It has a unique maximum element {(1,n+1),(2,n+2),...,(n,2n)}, and its minimal elements are the noncrossing matchings, counted by the Catalan number (1/(n+1)) binom(2n,n).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper states these as basic properties of the uncrossing poset. The rank function is the crossing number: each cover relation is a local uncrossing and lowers c by one, while the displayed maximum has every pair of its n wires crossing, hence binom(n,2) crossings. The minimal elements are precisely the matchings with no crossings left to resolve, which are the usual noncrossing matchings on 2n points, counted by the Catalan number.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1, after Lam
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let hat P_n be the poset obtained from P_n by adjoining a unique minimum element hat0 below all matchings, with c(hat0)=-1. Then hat P_n is Eulerian: for every nontrivial interval in hat P_n, the number of elements of even rank equals the number of elements of odd rank.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This theorem is cited from Lam and is not reproved in this paper. The paper summarizes Lam's proof as follows. Lam uses a map τ ↦ g_τ from matchings to affine permutations of type (n,2n). First, for intervals [τ,η] contained in P_n, he proves equality of the numbers of odd-rank and even-rank elements by descending induction on c(τ)+c(η). Then he handles intervals [hat0,η] by constructing an involution σ ↦ s_i·σ on the set {σ ∈ (hat0,η] : s_i·σ ≠ σ}, which pairs elements of opposite parity.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 11
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let tilde S_{2n} be the affine permutation group with simple generators s_0,s_1,...,s_{2n-1}. For τ ∈ P_n, write τ(i) for the point matched with i and define h_τ(i)=τ(i)-n if i<τ(i), and h_τ(i)=τ(i)+n if i>τ(i). Let φ(τ)=[h_τ(1),h_τ(2),...,h_τ(2n)] in window notation. An affine permutation w ∈ tilde S_{2n} is modular palindromic if it has a reduced decomposition w=s_{i_1}s_{i_2}...s_{i_{2k}} such that |i_{2k+1-r}-i_r|=n for every r∈[k]. Let MP_n be the set of modular palindromic affine permutations for which no reduced word contains, as a not-necessarily-adjacent subword, n consecutive generator indices. Then φ(P_n)⊆MP_n.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof has two parts. First, modular palindromicity is proved by decreasing induction on the rank of τ. The maximum matching hat1={(1,n+1),...,(n,2n)} maps to the identity, which is modular palindromic. Suppose τ is already known to map to MP_n and choose a crossing between wires (a,τ(a)) and (b,τ(b)) with a<b<τ(a)<τ(b). If the crossing is resolved to (a,τ(b)),(b,τ(a)), then in window notation φ(τ′) is obtained from φ(τ) by swapping the entries in positions a and b and also swapping the values a+n and b+n; equivalently φ(τ′)=t_{a+n,b+n} φ(τ) t_{a,b}, where t_{p,q} is the periodic transposition. The other resolution is handled similarly using the paired transpositions t_{b-n,τ(a)-n} and t_{b,τ(a)}. These paired operations preserve the modular palindromic form. Second, to prove the no-consecutive-subword condition, the paper argues by contradiction. If some reduced word for φ(τ) contained a subword of n consecutive indices, take a maximal such subword, say s_{a+1}s_{a+2}...s_{a+m} with m≥n, after possibly reversing orientation and interpreting indices modulo 2n. Then φ(τ)(a+m+1)=a+1. But directly from the definition of h_τ one has i-n<φ(τ)(i)<i+n for every i∈[2n], contradicting m≥n.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 12
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: The map φ:P_n→MP_n is order-reversing. Equivalently, if τ′≤τ in the uncrossing poset P_n, then φ(τ′)≥φ(τ) in Bruhat order on MP_n, viewed as an induced subposet of tilde S_{2n}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: It suffices to check a cover relation, where τ′ is obtained by resolving one crossing of τ. In the proof of Lemma 11, resolving a crossing changes φ(τ) by multiplying on the two sides by paired periodic transpositions, so a reduced word for φ(τ) appears as a subword of a reduced word for φ(τ′). By the subword characterization of Bruhat order, φ(τ)≤φ(τ′). Taking transitive closure gives the result.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 13
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let p,q∈MP_n satisfy q=s_a p s_{a+n} and ℓ(q)=ℓ(p)+2, where ℓ is affine Coxeter length and indices are read modulo 2n. Let x,y∈[2n] be such that p(x)≡a mod 2n and p(y)≡a+1 mod 2n. Then: (1) the four numbers x, y, a+n, and a+n+1 are distinct; (2) q(i)=p(i) for every i∈[2n] outside {a+n,a+n+1,x,y}; and (3) q(a+n)=p(a+n+1), q(a+n+1)=p(a+n), q(x)≡a+1 mod 2n, and q(y)≡a mod 2n.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The inequalities i-n<p(i)<i+n for elements of MP_n imply immediately that x≠y, a+n≠a+n+1, a+n≠x, and a+n+1≠y. If y=a+n, then p(a+n)≡a+1, forcing p to contain a subword s_{a+1}s_{a+2}...s_{a+n-1}; multiplying to form q=s_a p s_{a+n} would then create a forbidden subword of n consecutive indices. The case x=a+n+1 is analogous, using the decreasing consecutive subword s_{a+2n+1}s_{a+2n}...s_{a+n+1}. This proves distinctness. For i outside the four exceptional positions, right multiplication by s_{a+n} does not move i and left multiplication by s_a does not change p(i), so q(i)=p(i). The displayed formulas in (3) follow directly from the fact that right multiplication by s_{a+n} swaps the positions a+n and a+n+1, while left multiplication by s_a swaps the values congruent to a and a+1.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 10
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: The map φ:P_n→MP_n defined by φ(τ)=[h_τ(1),...,h_τ(2n)], with h_τ(i)=τ(i)-n for i<τ(i) and h_τ(i)=τ(i)+n for i>τ(i), is an order-reversing bijection from the uncrossing poset P_n to the induced Bruhat subposet MP_n.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Injectivity is immediate from the definition: the mate τ(i) is recovered from φ(τ)(i) by shifting by n in the appropriate direction. Lemma 11 shows φ(P_n)⊆MP_n, and Corollary 12 shows the order-reversing property. The main work is surjectivity. Given p∈MP_n, define τ_p(i)=p(i)+n if p(i)≤n, and τ_p(i)=p(i)-n if p(i)≥n+1. The displacement inequality i-n<p(i)<i+n implies τ_p(i)≠i. The paper then proves τ_p is an involution by induction on ℓ(p)/2. One rewrites τ_p^2(i) as the congruence τ_p^2(i)≡p(p(i)+n)+n mod 2n, with the representative in [2n]. For p=e this gives τ_e^2(i)=i. For the induction step, write q=s_a p s_{a+n} with ℓ(q)=ℓ(p)+2. Let x,y be the preimages of a and a+1 modulo 2n under p. Lemma 13 says q differs from p only at x,y,a+n,a+n+1 and describes the differences. For all other i, q(q(i)+n)+n≡s_a(p(p(i)+n))+n≡s_a(i+n)+n≡i+2n, and the four exceptional cases are checked directly. Thus τ_q^2(i)=i for all i. Hence τ_p is a fixed-point-free involution, i.e. a matching, and by construction φ(τ_p)=p.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 14, Ehrenborg-Readdy
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let P be an Eulerian poset with unique minimum hat0 and unique maximum hat1, and let Pyr(P) denote the product of P with a two-element chain. If Φ_P(c,d) denotes the cd-index of P, then Φ_Pyr(P)=1/2(Φ_P·c + c·Φ_P + ∑_{x:hat0<x<hat1} Φ_[hat0,x]·d·Φ_[x,hat1]), where the sum ranges over all proper non-extreme elements x of P.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper cites this proposition from Ehrenborg and Readdy and uses it as a black box. Its role in the paper is to compute the cd-index of Q_0=[u,w]×[e,ss′], the product of an interval with a two-element chain, before subsequent zipping operations are applied.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 15, Reading zipping theorem
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let P be a poset with unique minimum and maximum, and let x,y,z be a zipper in P: x,y,z are distinct, the strict lower sets of x and y are equal, z=x∨y covers x and y, and z covers no other element. Form P′ by deleting x,y,z, inserting one new element xy, putting a≤b as before among undeleted elements, declaring xy≤a when x≤a or y≤a in P, and declaring a≤xy when a≤x equivalently a≤y in P. Then P′ is a poset. If P is Eulerian, then P′ is Eulerian. Moreover, P has a cd-index if and only if P′ has a cd-index, and Φ_P′=Φ_P−Φ_[hat0,x]·d·Φ_[z,hat1].
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper cites this result from Reading and uses it as the mechanism for passing from the product interval [u,w]×[e,ss′] to the interval [u,sws′]. The operation can be visualized as deleting the top z of a diamond-like configuration and identifying its two lower elements x and y. The cd-index changes by exactly the product of the cd-indices of the lower interval up to x, the single d, and the upper interval above z.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 16, Reading structural recursion for Bruhat intervals
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let (W,S) be a Coxeter system, let u,w∈W, and let s∈S. Assume ws>w, us>u, and u≤w in Bruhat order. If us∉[u,w], then [u,ws] is isomorphic to [u,w]×[1,s] and [us,ws] is isomorphic to [u,w]. If us∈[u,w], then [u,ws] can be obtained from [u,w]×[1,s] by a sequence of zipping operations.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper cites this theorem from Reading as the prototype for its interval recursion in MP_n. The theorem uses the behavior of right multiplication by a simple reflection and the lifting property in Bruhat order to compare [u,w], [us,ws], and [u,ws]. The product with [1,s] represents adding one binary choice, and the zippings account for collisions where two choices map to the same Bruhat element.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 17, Reading cd-index recursion for Bruhat intervals
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let (W,S) be a Coxeter system, let u,w∈W, and let s∈S. Assume u<us, w<ws, and u≤w. For v∈W set σ_s(v)=ℓ(vs)−ℓ(v), so σ_s(v)∈{−1,1}. If us∉[u,w], then Φ_[us,ws]=Φ_[u,w] and Φ_[u,ws]=Φ_Pyr([u,w])=1/2(Φ_[u,w]·c+c·Φ_[u,w]+∑_{v:u<v<w} Φ_[u,v]·d·Φ_[v,w]). If us∈[u,w], then Φ_[u,ws]=Φ_Pyr([u,w])−∑_{v:u<v<w, vs<v} Φ_[u,v]·d·Φ_[v,w]=1/2(Φ_[u,w]·c+c·Φ_[u,w]+∑_{v:u<v<w} σ_s(v)Φ_[u,v]·d·Φ_[v,w]).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This is cited from Reading and motivates Theorem 18. The first case is the cd-index of a pyramid/product interval. In the second case, the interval is obtained from the product by zipping exactly those triples corresponding to elements v for which vs<v; applying the zipping formula subtracts Φ_[u,v]·d·Φ_[v,w] for each such v. Combining this with the pyramid formula converts the expression into the signed sum using σ_s(v).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 21, Bruhat lifting property
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let (W,S) be a Coxeter system, let u,w∈W, and let s∈S. If w>ws and us>u, then the following are equivalent in Bruhat order: (i) w>u; (ii) ws>u; (iii) w>us.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper quotes this standard lifting property from Björner and Brenti. It is used as the Coxeter-theoretic input for proving the analogous lifting property inside MP_n and for checking order preservation of the map η.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 22, lifting property for MP_n
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let u,w∈MP_n, let s=s_i be a simple generator of tilde S_{2n}, and let s′=s_{i+n}. If w>sws′ and sus′>u in MP_n, then the following are equivalent: (i) w>u; (ii) sws′>u; and (iii) w>sus′.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The implications (ii)⇒(i) and (iii)⇒(i) are immediate by transitivity. For (i)⇒(ii),(iii), choose a modular palindromic reduced decomposition sws′=t_1t_2...t_{2q}. Since w>sws′, the word w=s t_1t_2...t_{2q} s′ is also reduced and modular palindromic. If u≤w, then u has a reduced decomposition appearing as a subword of this word. The assumption sus′>u prevents the chosen subword for u from using the initial s and the final s′. Hence the subword already lies in t_1...t_{2q}, giving u≤sws′, and adjoining the initial and final generators gives sus′≤w.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 23
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let u,w∈MP_n, let s=s_i and s′=s_{i+n}, and assume u<sus′ and w<sws′. Define η:[u,w]×[e,ss′]→[u,sws′] by η(v,e)=v, and η(v,ss′)=svs′ if svs′>v, while η(v,ss′)=v if svs′<v. Then η is a well-defined, surjective, order-preserving map.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Well-definedness follows because v∈[u,w] implies v∈[u,sws′], and if svs′>v then u≤v<svs′<sws′ by the MP_n lifting property. For order preservation, compare (v_1,a_1)≤(v_2,a_2) in the product. If a_1=a_2=e, the claim is immediate. If a_1=e and a_2=ss′, then η(v_2,ss′) is either v_2 or sv_2s′, both above v_1. If a_1=a_2=ss′, there are four cases depending on whether sv_js′ is above or below v_j. The two easy cases use transitivity; the mixed case η(v_1,ss′)=sv_1s′ and η(v_2,ss′)=v_2 uses the MP_n lifting property to get sv_1s′≤v_2; and the case where both images are sv_js′ again uses lifting to get sv_1s′≤sv_2s′. The paper refers to Reading's analogous proposition for the proof of surjectivity.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 24
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Assume u<sus′ and w<sws′ in MP_n. Let Z={v∈MP_n : u<v<w and svs′<v}, and list Z as v_1,...,v_k with weakly increasing Coxeter lengths. Let Q_0=[u,w]×[e,ss′]. Recursively, let Q_i be obtained from Q_{i-1} by zipping the triple {(v_i,e),(s v_i s′,ss′),(v_i,ss′)}. Then for every 1≤i≤k, the triple {(v_i,e),(s v_i s′,ss′),(v_i,ss′)} is a zipper in Q_{i-1}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: First the paper checks that the three elements have not already been removed or identified before stage i. The element (v_i,ss′) has not yet appeared in a lower-rank zipping; the elements (v_i,e) and (s v_i s′,ss′) are not of the form deleted in earlier zippings, except that they will be identified with each other at the current step. Next it proves equality of the lower sets of (v_i,e) and (s v_i s′,ss′). If (x,a)<(v_i,e), then a=e and x<v_i. If x did not satisfy x<sxs′, then a zipper involving x would already have occurred at a smaller length, impossible; hence x<sxs′, and the MP_n lifting property gives x≤s v_i s′, so (x,e)<(s v_i s′,ss′). Conversely, if (x,a)<(s v_i s′,ss′), then either a=e, in which case x≤s v_i s′<v_i, or a=ss′. In the latter case x<s v_i s′ has smaller length, and if it occurred it would likewise force an earlier zipper, so it is ruled out. Thus the strict lower sets coincide. The element (v_i,ss′) plainly covers (v_i,e) and (s v_i s′,ss′). If it covered another (x,a), then either a=e and x=v_i, or a=ss′ and x⋖v_i; in the second case the lifting property and x<sxs′ force x=s v_i s′. Therefore it covers no other elements, so the triple is a zipper.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 26 and Corollary 27
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let u,w∈MP_n, let s=s_i and s′=s_{i+n}, and assume u<sus′, w<sws′, and u≤w. If sus′ is not in the interval [u,w], equivalently sus′ not ≤ w under these hypotheses, then svs′>v for every v∈[u,w], and the map η:[u,w]×[e,ss′]→[u,sws′] from Proposition 23 is an order-preserving bijection. Consequently, θ:[u,w]→[sus′,sws′] defined by θ(v)=svs′ is an order-preserving bijection.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper states this as the MP_n analogue of Reading's Proposition 5.2 and omits the proof. Conceptually, if some v∈[u,w] had svs′<v, then applying the lifting behavior would force sus′≤w, contradicting the hypothesis. Hence η never identifies the e and ss′ layers and becomes bijective. Restricting to the ss′ layer gives the interval isomorphism θ.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 28
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let u,w∈MP_n, let s=s_i and s′=s_{i+n}, and assume w<sws′, u<sus′, and u≤w. If sus′∉[u,w], then [u,sws′] is isomorphic to [u,w]×[e,ss′], and [sus′,sws′] is isomorphic to [u,w]. If sus′∈[u,w], then [u,sws′] can be obtained from [u,w]×[e,ss′] by a sequence of zipping operations.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The first case follows directly from Proposition 26 and Corollary 27: η gives an order-preserving bijection from the product to [u,sws′], and θ gives the copy [sus′,sws′]≅[u,w]. In the second case, Proposition 23 gives a surjective order-preserving map η from the product to [u,sws′]. The elements where η has nontrivial fibers are precisely the triples indexed by Z={v:u<v<w and svs′<v}. Ordering Z by weakly increasing length and applying Proposition 24, each such fiber is collapsed by a valid zipper. Performing all these zippings gives the target interval.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 18
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let u,w∈MP_n, let s=s_i and s′=s_{i+n}, and assume u<sus′, w<sws′, and u≤w. For v∈MP_n define σ_s(v)=1/2(ℓ(svs′)−ℓ(v)), so σ_s(v)∈{−1,1}. If sus′∉[u,w], then Φ_[sus′,sws′]=Φ_[u,w], and Φ_[u,sws′]=Φ_Pyr([u,w])=1/2(Φ_[u,w]·c+c·Φ_[u,w]+∑_{v∈MP_n, u<v<w} Φ_[u,v]·d·Φ_[v,w]). If sus′∈[u,w], then Φ_[u,sws′]=Φ_Pyr([u,w])−∑_{v∈MP_n, u<v<w, svs′<v} Φ_[u,v]·d·Φ_[v,w]=1/2(Φ_[u,w]·c+c·Φ_[u,w]+∑_{v∈MP_n, u<v<w} σ_s(v)Φ_[u,v]·d·Φ_[v,w]).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The first case uses Theorem 28: [u,sws′] is the product of [u,w] with a two-element chain, and [sus′,sws′] is a copy of [u,w]. Applying the Ehrenborg-Readdy pyramid formula gives the displayed expression. For the second case, define the sequence of posets Q_i from Proposition 24, starting with Q_0=[u,w]×[e,ss′] and ending with Q_k=[u,sws′]. For the i-th zipper, Reading's zipping formula gives Φ_{Q_{i-1}}−Φ_{Q_i}=Φ_{[(u,e),(v_i,e)]}·d·Φ_{[(v_i,ss′),(w,ss′)]}, where the intervals are taken inside Q_{i-1}. These intervals are naturally isomorphic to [u,v_i] and [v_i,w]. Summing over all v_i with sv_i s′<v_i yields the subtraction formula. Finally, replacing Φ_{Q_0} by the pyramid formula and observing that σ_s(v)=1 for svs′>v and σ_s(v)=-1 for svs′<v gives the signed half-sum expression.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 29
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let P be a graded poset of rank n with a unique maximum element hat1 and possibly multiple minimal elements. Assume every interval in P is Eulerian. Let hat P be obtained by adjoining a unique minimum element hat0 with rank ρ(hat0)=-1, and assume hat P is Eulerian. Then the ab-index of hat P is Ψ_hatP(a,b)=(a−b)^n + ∑_{i=0}^{n-1} (a−b)^i b ∑_{ρ(x)=i} Ψ_[x,hat1](a,b).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof starts from the chain formula for the ab-index after the substitution Ψ_hatP(a+b,b). A chain hat0<t_1<...<t_{k-1}<hat1 contributes a monomial a^{ρ(hat0,t_1)-1} b a^{ρ(t_1,t_2)-1} b ... b a^{ρ(t_{k-1},hat1)-1}; substituting a−b for a gives the displayed form for Ψ_hatP(a,b). The sum is then grouped by the first element t_1 above hat0. If there is no such t_1, the contribution is (a−b)^n. If ρ(t_1)=i, the initial segment contributes (a−b)^i b and the remaining part of the chain contributes exactly Ψ_[t_1,hat1](a,b). Summing over all t_1 of rank i gives the formula.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 30
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: The ab-index of hat P_n is recursively given by Ψ_hatP_n(a,b)=(a−b)^{binom(n,2)} + ∑_{i=0}^{binom(n,2)-1} (a−b)^i b ∑_{x∈MP_n, ℓ(x)=2 binom(n,2)-2i} Ψ_[e,x](a,b), where ℓ is affine Coxeter length. More generally, if τ∈P_n has c(τ)=k≤binom(n,2) and φ(τ)=w∈MP_n, then the ab-index of the interval [hat0,τ]⊂hat P_n is Ψ_[hat0,τ](a,b)=(a−b)^k + ∑_{i=0}^{k-1} (a−b)^i b ∑_{x∈MP_n, x>w, ℓ(x)=2 binom(n,2)-2i} Ψ_[w,x](a,b).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This is an application of Proposition 29 to hat P_n and to intervals [hat0,τ]. Theorem 10 identifies elements below a matching τ with elements x above w=φ(τ) in MP_n, because φ is order-reversing. The rank c(σ) of a matching σ corresponds to affine length ℓ(φ(σ))=2 binom(n,2)-2 c(σ). Substituting this correspondence into the first-element decomposition of Proposition 29 gives both displayed recursions. The intervals [w,x] in MP_n have ab-indices obtainable from the cd-index recursions of Theorem 18 via Ψ(a,b)=Φ(a+b,ab+ba).

proof_techniques.name: Affine-permutation encoding of uncrossing matchings
proof_techniques.description: The paper replaces a matching τ by an affine permutation φ(τ) whose window entries record the mate of each endpoint shifted by ±n according to whether the mate lies to the right or left. The image is characterized intrinsically as MP_n, the modular palindromic affine permutations with no reduced word containing n consecutive generator indices. This turns the combinatorics of resolving crossings into Bruhat-order comparisons, with φ reversing the uncrossing order.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If the matchings allowed by a fixed word w form a subcollection of complete matchings stable under local resolution, one can try to view it as a subposet or union of intervals inside P_n and then transfer it through φ to an affine-Bruhat setting. This could give access to rank functions, interval decompositions, and known Bruhat-order tools for studying the face poset of the cubical complex F_w.

proof_techniques.name: Resolving a crossing as adding paired simple reflections
proof_techniques.description: In the φ-model, resolving a crossing in a matching corresponds to multiplying the associated affine permutation by paired transpositions whose simple-reflection words are shifted by n from one another. This is why the relevant affine permutations have modular palindromic reduced words, and why Bruhat length changes in even increments.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The cells of F_w are cubes whose facets are obtained by resolving individual crossings. A useful local model may be to encode one crossing resolution as a paired operation, so that resolving any subset of crossings corresponds to moving in a Bruhat-like interval or in a quotient of such an interval.

proof_techniques.name: Product-with-chain plus zipping decomposition
proof_techniques.description: The core recursive structure is: after choosing a paired generator s,s′, the interval [u,sws′] is compared to [u,w]×[e,ss′]. If no collision occurs, it is exactly this product; if collisions occur, they are resolved by a sequence of zipping operations, each deleting the top of a small diamond and identifying its two lower elements. The zipping sequence is indexed by elements v with svs′<v.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The construction resembles building a cubical complex one crossing at a time: product with a two-element chain corresponds to adding a binary resolution choice, while zipping corresponds to identifying configurations that yield the same resolved matching. This may be a template for proving contractibility by recursive decomposition, quotienting, or controlled collapses.

proof_techniques.name: Lifting property adapted to the matching subposet
proof_techniques.description: The paper proves an MP_n analogue of the Bruhat lifting property: under w>sws′ and sus′>u, the inequalities w>u, sws′>u, and w>sus′ are equivalent. The proof uses modular palindromic reduced decompositions and the subword criterion for Bruhat order.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: A lifting property for whatever labeled or word-constrained matching poset underlies F_w would help control whether resolving or adding a chosen crossing keeps one inside the relevant interval. Such control is essential for recursive arguments on cells and their attaching maps.

proof_techniques.name: No-long-consecutive-subword displacement criterion
proof_techniques.description: The condition that an affine permutation in MP_n has no reduced word containing n consecutive generator indices implies a concrete displacement inequality i−n<p(i)<i+n for every window position i. This inequality lets the paper reconstruct a genuine matching from an affine permutation by shifting values by n, and it rules out pathological affine permutations that are modular palindromic but not matchings.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For constrained matchings, a similar intrinsic characterization of valid states could be valuable: instead of tracking embedded arcs directly, one might characterize allowable resolution states by inequalities or forbidden patterns in an algebraic encoding.

proof_techniques.name: Surjectivity by reconstructing an involution
proof_techniques.description: To prove φ is onto MP_n, the paper defines τ_p(i)=p(i)+n or p(i)-n depending on the window value p(i), then proves τ_p is a fixed-point-free involution. The involution property is shown by induction on half the affine length; the induction step isolates exactly four exceptional positions affected by q=s_a p s_{a+n}, using Lemma 13.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is a useful pattern for proving that an algebraically defined object really encodes a matching: define candidate partners explicitly, prove no fixed points by a displacement inequality, and prove involutivity by tracking a local move with finitely many exceptional positions.

proof_techniques.name: Flag-enumerative recursion from first chain element
proof_techniques.description: Proposition 29 computes the ab-index of a poset with a new minimum by grouping chains according to their first element above the new minimum. This reduces global flag enumeration to the ab-indices of ordinary intervals above elements of fixed rank.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If one attacks F_w via Euler characteristic or flag enumeration of a face poset, this chain-decomposition method may help express global counts in terms of local resolution intervals.

proof_techniques.name: Eulerian parity via involutions
proof_techniques.description: Although Lam's proof is only summarized, the paper records that Eulerianity of hat P_n is proved by pairing odd- and even-rank elements in intervals using affine-permutation operations, after handling intervals inside P_n by descending induction.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For F_w, a sign-reversing involution on cells or on unmatched resolution states could help compute Euler characteristic or verify local sphere/ball behavior in links, which are common steps in contractibility proofs.

other_useful_info: The paper notes that Lam's earlier map τ↦g_τ identifies P_n with an induced subposet of dual affine Bruhat order of type (n,2n), while Kim's modified φ lands in the full affine permutation group tilde S_{2n} and gives an explicit image MP_n. It also notes that Lam's work on electrical networks gives cell structures described by uncrossing posets, suggesting a broader precedent for topological cell complexes governed by crossing-resolution orders. Remark 19 states without proof an additional recursion for Φ_[sus′,sws′] in the case sus′∈[u,w] using Reading's vertex-shaving operation Shv. The examples in the paper show small intervals in MP_n that are Boolean lattices or isomorphic to the Bruhat order of S_3, illustrating that local uncrossing intervals can have familiar combinatorial types.
why_relevant: Analyzes intervals in the uncrossing order and gives recursive and order-reversing descriptions. Interval structure is likely relevant because cells of F_w are attached by resolving subsets of crossings.
